title: An Opportunity to Bow Before Justice!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: When I became Chief Minister under the DMK government in 1989, 13,500 People's Welfare Workers were appointed on 2 July 1990, with the good intention of creating employment and a livelihood for unemployed young people. For the sole reason that they had been appointed under the DMK, the AIADMK government dismissed every one of them as soon as it came to power in 1991.

When the DMK returned under my leadership in 1996, all those sent home were given their jobs back. The AIADMK returned in 2001 and dismissed them again on 31 May. When the DMK formed its fifth government under my leadership in 2006, they were reappointed on 31 May. As soon as the AIADMK took office in May 2011, it repeated its earlier action and issued a government order dismissing them all on 8 November 2011.

Union office-bearers challenged the dismissal in the High Court. Justice Suguna granted an interim stay. When the case came up on 21 November 2011, the court was told its order had not been obeyed and the workers had not been reinstated. Justice Suguna ordered their immediate reinstatement and directed that a compliance report be filed on 23 November.

An election to the union was held on 3 April 2012, choosing Mathivanan as president and Rajendran as secretary. Any major union decision had to be taken by its Executive Committee. Yet Palani, a former secretary who had already been removed, consulted no one and independently entered an agreement accepting dismissal in return for five months' salary. This was placed before the court, which accepted it as genuine and approved the dismissal.
Only afterwards did senior advocate Wilson, representing the union, tell the court that Palani no longer held office, Mathivanan did, and the agreement with Palani must be cancelled.

The State appealed against Justice Suguna's order. The First Bench of Chief Justice Iqbal and Justice Sivagnanam said the dismissals appeared to have been carried out according to the government's whim. Even if the workers were considered temporary for argument's sake, they had served for twenty years. Before removing them with one signature, the government had neither given notice nor heard their views as justice and law required.

The Bench held that Justice Suguna had correctly granted a stay and refused to remove it. The workers were being treated like a football. Once a person had been given government work with pay fixed under a Supreme Court order, removal without proper reason was unlawful. The judges strongly condemned the painful and deplorable practice of governments issuing opposite orders whenever power changed. If a new government altered an earlier scheme, it should place its workers in other jobs, not dismiss them. The judges recognised the political element but did not wish to enter it.

The Tamil Nadu government appealed to the Supreme Court. Hearing it on 29 November 2011, Justices D. K. Jain and Dave asked: "What is happening in Tamil Nadu? Why are the previous government's schemes cancelled whenever the government changes? Such things must not happen in Tamil Nadu again."

When the case returned on 11 November 2013, the Bench of Justices Anil Dave and Dipak Misra set aside the High Court order that had permitted dismissal. It directed reconsideration of every issue on its merits and completion within six months, without the Tamil Nadu government seeking adjournments. The Court said the government had ruined the lives of 13,000 workers by making an agreement with an unauthorised person and must remember that they had devoted twenty years of their lives to government service.

Honourable judges asked, "What is happening in Tamil Nadu?" and "Is it right to play with People's Welfare Workers like a ball?" Does this government feel no shame? Is it right to persist stubbornly for the sole reason that these workers received employment under the DMK? Did a DMK government ever send home anyone appointed in this manner under the AIADMK?

When the Supreme Court judgment appeared, I said: "Let what has happened remain in the past. At least now the AIADMK government must gain clarity and abandon vindictive action. Though the Supreme Court has directed the High Court to finish the case within six months, the State should not wait. It should withdraw the case and immediately restore work to all 13,000 People's Welfare Workers. The proverb says anger must cool. One must not forget that preserving anger without letting it cool leads to the abyss. The AIADMK government must calm its anger against these workers merely because the DMK appointed them, and come forward to protect them and their families."
But would Jayalalithaa listen to such an appeal?

Under the Supreme Court ruling, the Madras High Court reheard the case. Senior advocate Wilson explained that the DMK had appointed the workers according to rules, through a selection committee and newspaper advertisements; that the AIADMK dismissed them; and that political hostility alone motivated their removal.

After hearing both sides, Justices Paul Vasanthakumar and Sathyanarayanan delivered a fine judgment on 19 August 2014. They directed that the dismissed workers be employed, under the same or another title, in prohibition-awareness work or in government schools, village panchayats, municipalities, corporations, village administrative offices, taluk offices, Collectors' offices and other government offices. Their maximum age should be disregarded and work assigned according to educational qualifications, preferably in their own towns or districts.

The process was to begin immediately and finish by 31 October 2014. If any worker remained unappointed by then, the government was to pay that worker, from 1 November, the last monthly salary received. Since the Tamil Nadu government employed more than 29,000 people to earn about Rs 21,600 crore annually from liquor sales, the judges suggested employing the workers to explain alcohol's harmful effects through prohibition campaigns. They could also raise awareness through child-health and rural-sanitation programmes.

The AIADMK has governed for three and a half years. It wasted all that time without employing the workers and dragged them through suffering as far as the Supreme Court. Judges repeatedly condemned the government and firmly insisted that work be provided.

Even now, it must not delay. It should remember the tragedy that nineteen People's Welfare Workers died by suicide after losing employment, and that most of those suffering without work belong to Scheduled Caste, Backward and Most Backward communities, and employ them immediately.

If, merely because the DMK appointed them, the government disregards this judgment too and appeals again to cause further delay, there can be no greater cruelty or injustice. The people of Tamil Nadu know how excellent a reputation the AIADMK government generally enjoys among public employees, do they not?

At least now, respecting the court's judgment and treating the People's Welfare Workers humanely would be good for the government and good for the country. The AIADMK government generally respects neither fairness nor court judgments. Will it abandon that habit now and use this opportunity to bow before the High Court's judgment?
With affection,
M. K.
22 August 2014
